Season of television series

| ← **Previous** [Season 1](/wiki/Fringe_season_1 "Fringe season 1")  **Next** → [Season 3](/wiki/Fringe_season_3 "Fringe season 3") | |

The second season of the American [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") television series *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")* commenced airing on the [Fox network](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") on September 17, 2009, and concluded on May 20, 2010. The season was produced by [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot") in association with [Warner Bros. Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television"), and its [showrunners](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner") were [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner") and [J. H. Wyman](/wiki/J._H._Wyman "J. H. Wyman"). Actors [Anna Torv](/wiki/Anna_Torv "Anna Torv"), [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble"), and [Joshua Jackson](/wiki/Joshua_Jackson "Joshua Jackson") reprised their roles as FBI agent [Olivia Dunham](/wiki/Olivia_Dunham "Olivia Dunham") and father-son duo [Walter](/wiki/Walter_Bishop_(Fringe) "Walter Bishop (Fringe)") and [Peter Bishop](/wiki/Peter_Bishop "Peter Bishop"), respectively. Previous series regulars [Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick"), [Jasika Nicole](/wiki/Jasika_Nicole "Jasika Nicole"), [Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown"), and [Kirk Acevedo](/wiki/Kirk_Acevedo "Kirk Acevedo") also returned, though with Acevedo in a limited capacity.

The season followed the continuation of a war between two universes, the prime and the [parallel](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)"). It was set in the former, until the last several episodes when [Peter Bishop](/wiki/Peter_Bishop "Peter Bishop") (Jackson) journeyed back to the parallel universe after being lured there by his real father, ["Walternate"](/wiki/Walter_Bishop_(Fringe) "Walter Bishop (Fringe)") (Noble). While co-creator [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams") described the first season as "identifying that there is an enemy", he referred to season two as "getting to know the enemy" as it "build[s] to a very specific type of confrontation" between the two universes.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The writers focused on their characters' development, in particular making them more comfortable with each other while solving cases for Fringe Division. By inventing the "mythalone" style of episode, the producers sought to create the perfect episode that mixed standalone episode qualities for casual viewers with the further development of the series' mythology for regular viewers.

In a departure from the [previous season](/wiki/Fringe_season_1 "Fringe season 1"), the second season aired in a new competitive timeslot at 9:00 pm on Thursdays. It contained 22 episodes, plus an unaired episode that was produced during the first season; "[Unearthed](/wiki/Unearthed_(Fringe) "Unearthed (Fringe)")" aired as a special as episode 11 of season two, days prior to "[Johari Window](/wiki/Johari_Window_(Fringe) "Johari Window (Fringe)")", the first new episode of 2010. Also part of the season was the series' only musical episode, "[Brown Betty](/wiki/Brown_Betty_(Fringe) "Brown Betty (Fringe)")", which was produced at the request of the network. The season finale, "[Over There](/wiki/Over_There_(Fringe) "Over There (Fringe)")", fully introduced the parallel universe and laid the groundwork for the [third season](/wiki/Fringe_season_3 "Fringe season 3").

*Fringe* ended its second season with a per episode average of 6.252 million total viewers and a 2.3 ratings share in the 18–49 demographic. The season was generally well-received by critics, though most agreed that the second half was a considerable improvement over the first. The series was chosen for a number of 2010 "best of television" lists, including *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, the *[Seattle Post-Intelligencer](/wiki/Seattle_Post-Intelligencer "Seattle Post-Intelligencer")*, and *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*. Despite its critical acclaim, *Fringe* failed to earn any major category nominations at the [62nd Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/62nd_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "62nd Primetime Emmy Awards"), but did receive nominations at the [Creative Arts Emmy Awards](/wiki/Creative_Arts_Emmy_Award "Creative Arts Emmy Award"), [Golden Reel Awards](/wiki/Golden_Reel_Award_(Motion_Picture_Sound_Editors) "Golden Reel Award (Motion Picture Sound Editors)"), and [Satellite Awards](/wiki/Satellite_Award "Satellite Award"); at the [Saturn Awards](/wiki/Saturn_Award "Saturn Award"), Torv and guest actor [Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy") won in their respective categories. The second season was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc") in region 1 on September 14, 2010, in region 2 on September 27, and in region 4 on November 10.

## Season summary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Season summary")]

Olivia, having been taken to William Bell's office in the [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)"), is returned to the prime universe but with short term amnesia, unable to recall her experience there. Massive Dynamic's Nina Sharp directs her to Sam Weiss, a bowling alley manager, who gives her cryptic but helpful advice to overcome her amnesia. Meanwhile, the Fringe division has discovered several shapeshifters - a human/machine hybrid that bleed mercury - have crossed over, but unbeknownst to them, one takes the form of Olivia's partner, Charlie. When Sam's advice allows Olivia to recall what Bell told her, she unwittingly gives this information to the shapeshifter, who calls its agents to use the information to recover the body of Thomas Jerome Newton, an agent for some entity operating from the parallel universe. Nina provides Olivia with cautionary advice, alerting her to a "great war" that Bell postulated would occur between the two universes. During this time, Peter has come to forgive Walter for his past, and Walter has grown accustomed to normal life outside of the institution, but is still troubled by a secret.

Newton, using old technology from Walter and Bell's research, is able to pull an entire building from the parallel universe to the prime, and the team races against time to prevent harm when, due to the laws of mass conservation, a building from the prime is pulled to the parallel one. This event forces Walter to try to coax Olivia to recall her Cortexiphan abilities so as to identify things affected by the parallel universe. Initially unable to do so, her fear of failure enables her ability, allowing them to save the people within the target building, but also revealing to her that Peter is from the parallel universe. Walter explains to Olivia that in 1985, he and Bell had devised a way to observe the parallel universe, where he found his doppelgänger, "Walternate", was also close to losing his son. Though Walter's Peter had died, Walternate continued to search for a cure, but missed the telltale sign when the Observer September arrived at his lab. Walter resynthesized the cure and aimed to cross over using untested equipment at Reiden Lake, though stalled by Nina and his lab assistant Clara. Nina tackled him on his way over, losing her arm in the process, while Walter, on the other side, found the cure vial broken. Intent on curing the alternate Peter, he posed as his father and crossed back with Peter, intending to administer the cure at the lab and then return Peter. However, on the return, the ice on Reiden Lake broke, threatening to drown both, but they were saved by September, who cautioned Walter "the boy must live". While Walter successfully administered the cure, his wife Elizabeth saw Peter, and Walter realized he could never return Peter to the parallel universe. After Walter reveals this truth, he considers letting Peter know but struggles with how to do so, hoping to seek repentance from God for his actions.

Meanwhile, Newton has continued to use Walter's technology to bring into temporary existence elements from the parallel universe. This enables Newton and his agents to bring over a figure known as "Mr. Secretary", despite Fringe's attempts to stop them. Peter, from this action, deduces that he is from the parallel universe, and furious at Walter for hiding this information, leaves on his own. While hiding in the Pacific Northwest, Peter meets Mr. Secretary - Walternate, his true father, who offers to take him back to the parallel universe, which Peter accepts. Olivia and Walter are alerted by September that Walternate plans to use Peter to initiate the operation of a strange device that threatens to destroy the prime universe, and the two launch a rescue attempt. In the parallel universe, they find that it suffers from singularities caused by Walter's crossing in 1985, forcing Walternate's Fringe team to use an amber-like substance to surround and quarantine such areas, regardless of innocent lives trapped within. They meet with William Bell, and Walter and Bell resolve their past differences. Olivia faces off against her doppelgänger, "Fauxlivia", who works for Walternate in the Fringe Division under the U.S. Secretary of Defense; she is able to recover Peter, who has seen the device and recognized that it reacted only to his biology, and wants nothing of it, willing to return with the others. As Olivia, Walter, and Peter attempt to return, they are engaged by Fauxlivia and others in the Fringe Division. Bell sacrifices himself to provide energy into a device to allow the three to cross over, but none of them are aware that Fauxlivia has secretly switched places with Olivia under Walternate's orders, while Olivia is captured and held in a secured facility by Walternate.

## Episodes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Episodes")]

See also: [List of Fringe episodes](/wiki/List_of_Fringe_episodes "List of Fringe episodes")

| No. overall | No. in season | Title | Directed by | Written by | Original release date | Prod. code | US viewers (millions) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 21 | 1 | "[A New Day in the Old Town](/wiki/A_New_Day_in_the_Old_Town "A New Day in the Old Town")" | [Akiva Goldsman](/wiki/Akiva_Goldsman "Akiva Goldsman") | [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams") & Akiva Goldsman | September 17, 2009 (2009-09-17) | 3X5101 | 7.82[[2]](#cite_note-2) |
| Olivia's SUV is discovered after a car accident in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), however, Olivia is not inside. Minutes later, she suddenly ejects through the windshield having traveled to the [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)") and is hospitalized with brain damage. FBI agent Amy Jessup ([Meghan Markle](/wiki/Meghan_Markle "Meghan Markle")) takes an interest in Fringe Division and begins to investigate deeper. The male driver of the car that crashed into Dunham's is revealed to be a [shapeshifter](/wiki/Shapeshifter "Shapeshifter") 'soldier' from the parallel universe, and is ordered to question Olivia then kill her. When Olivia suddenly awakens from the coma she cannot remember any details and the shapeshifter (now in the form of a female nurse) tries to kill her. Agent Jessup interrupts this, and the shapeshifter escapes into the basement, where Agent Francis appears to shoot the shapeshifter. Broyles travels to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") to defend the Fringe Division, where Peter hands him the shapeshifter's device so Broyles can justify the need for the Division. At the end, it is revealed the shapeshifter had killed Agent Francis and assumed his form. | | | | | | | |
| 22 | 2 | "[Night of Desirable Objects](/wiki/Night_of_Desirable_Objects_(Fringe) "Night of Desirable Objects (Fringe)")" | [Brad Anderson](/wiki/Brad_Anderson_(director) "Brad Anderson (director)") | [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner") & [J. H. Wyman](/wiki/J._H._Wyman "J. H. Wyman") | September 24, 2009 (2009-09-24) | 3X5102 | 5.73[[3]](#cite_note-3) |
| Fringe Division investigates a missing road worker who was pulled to a tunnel filled with the remains of six other people who disappeared suddenly. Meanwhile Olivia, now released from the hospital, begins to develop super-hearing, and Nina Sharp advises her to meet Sam Weiss ([Kevin Corrigan](/wiki/Kevin_Corrigan "Kevin Corrigan")), a man who can begin to help with the side-effects. The investigation leads to nearby resident Andre Hughes ([John Savage](/wiki/John_Savage_(actor) "John Savage (actor)")), who lost his wife and baby in childbirth. When the bodies are [exhumed](/wiki/Burial#Exhumation "Burial"), they find the baby casket empty. Walter then learns that Hughes genetically altered the baby by incorporating mole and scorpion DNA. When Olivia and Peter enter the basement of the Hughes family house, they are attacked by the now adult baby, but the tunnel collapses, causing a police car to crush the hybrid. | | | | | | | |
| 23 | 3 | "[Fracture](/wiki/Fracture_(Fringe) "Fracture (Fringe)")" | [Bryan Spicer](/wiki/Bryan_Spicer "Bryan Spicer") | [David Wilcox](/wiki/David_Wilcox_(screenwriter) "David Wilcox (screenwriter)") | October 1, 2009 (2009-10-01) | 3X5103 | 6.03[[4]](#cite_note-4) |
| A secret military experiment is turning people into human bombs. After one such bomb detonated in a train station, Peter and Olivia travel to [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") to meet one of Peter's contacts who was involved in the project. They find a list of names from the experiment, the victim in the train station being one of them. They return to find the surviving members, and are able to prevent the next subject from exploding. They find the man responsible ([Stephen McHattie](/wiki/Stephen_McHattie "Stephen McHattie")) and bring him into custody; the man suggests the bombs were intended to eliminate agents working for the Observer. Also, Weiss cures Olivia's inability to walk without a cane. | | | | | | | |
| 24 | 4 | "[Momentum Deferred](/wiki/Momentum_Deferred "Momentum Deferred")" | [Joe Chappelle](/wiki/Joe_Chappelle "Joe Chappelle") | [Zack Stentz](/wiki/Zack_Stentz "Zack Stentz") & [Ashley Edward Miller](/wiki/Ashley_Miller_(screenwriter) "Ashley Miller (screenwriter)") | October 8, 2009 (2009-10-08) | 3X5104 | 5.83[[5]](#cite_note-5) |
| A group of shapeshifters are stealing human heads, and it is revealed they are looking for a specific one. Walter visits the woman ([Theresa Russell](/wiki/Theresa_Russell "Theresa Russell")) he experimented on with psychedelics to see what she might still remember about the shapeshifters from her visions; she volunteers to help. After being drugged, she sees the shapeshifters again. Meanwhile, Olivia remembers her meeting with William Bell ([Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy")). Bell explains what the shapeshifters are looking for, and states she is the only person able to stop "The Storm". Olivia meets Nina, who explains that "The Storm" is an event that Bell theorized; when two universes merge, only one will remain in existence. This is what the shapeshifters are trying to cause. Later, Olivia discovers Charlie is dead and that a shapeshifter has replaced him, and kills him. The shapeshifters find the head they are looking for, and attach it to its body. | | | | | | | |
| 25 | 5 | "[Dream Logic](/wiki/Dream_Logic "Dream Logic")" | Paul Edwards | [Josh Singer](/wiki/Josh_Singer "Josh Singer") | October 15, 2009 (2009-10-15) | 3X5105 | 5.78[[6]](#cite_note-6) |
| In [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle") a man attacks his boss believing he is a demon. Walter finds a computer chip attached to the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"), the part of the brain controlling dreams. Broyles and Sharp reveal new information leading to the sleep researcher who implanted the chips. Walter changes his theory; the dreams are being stolen from their hosts to cause a "[high](/wiki/Substance_intoxication "Substance intoxication")" in the researcher, who is receiving them, and who has two personalities. His doctor personality sets a trap so that Olivia and Peter will catch his alternate persona. Meanwhile, Olivia is grieving for Charlie, murdered by a shapeshifter in the previous episode, and Sam helps her work through it by reminding Olivia of her first meeting with Charlie. | | | | | | | |
| 26 | 6 | "[Earthling](/wiki/Earthling_(Fringe) "Earthling (Fringe)")" | [Jon Cassar](/wiki/Jon_Cassar "Jon Cassar") | J. H. Wyman & [Jeff Vlaming](/wiki/Jeff_Vlaming "Jeff Vlaming") | November 5, 2009 (2009-11-05) | 3X5106 | 4.86[[7]](#cite_note-7) |
| The Fringe Division is flummoxed by a case where victims are inexplicably disintegrated into ash. Broyles takes a particular interest in the case, as he worked on it in the past. He says that everyone who died had recently been to a hospital. The investigation leads the team to Russia, where they learn Fringe science is taking place in other countries as well. A man sent to space came back with a dark entity in him, which is stealing people's natural radiation, and killing them. Walter learns that the man and the entity are connected and cannot be separated, so Broyles kills the man to end the killings. ## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Development")]

### Crew

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Crew")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Jeff_Pinkner_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Jeff_Pinkner_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeff_Pinkner_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/J._H._Wyman_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-J._H._Wyman_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:J._H._Wyman_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

[Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner") (left) and [J.H. Wyman](/wiki/J.H._Wyman "J.H. Wyman") (right) returned as [showrunners](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner") and executive producers of *Fringe*.

The season was produced by [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot") in association with [Warner Bros. Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-ctribunemadmen-26) Though still set in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), the show's production for the second season moved from [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") out of financial necessity, as it lost access to New York's [TV production tax incentive program](/wiki/Movie_production_incentives_in_the_United_States "Movie production incentives in the United States").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) *Fringe* consequently got a mostly new writing staff and production team, though co-creators [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"), [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci"), and [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman"), and producer [Bryan Burk](/wiki/Bryan_Burk "Bryan Burk") remained involved with the series.[[29]](#cite_note-awn1-29) [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner") and [J.H. Wyman](/wiki/J.H._Wyman "J.H. Wyman") returned as executive producers and [showrunners](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner").[[29]](#cite_note-awn1-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Jeff Vlaming](/wiki/Jeff_Vlaming "Jeff Vlaming") and previous episode director [Akiva Goldsman](/wiki/Akiva_Goldsman "Akiva Goldsman") joined the crew as consulting producers.[[31]](#cite_note-sfscopepremiere-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) While Abrams had six episode writing credits for the first season he remained much more hands-off for the second, instead focusing on other commitments like the film *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(2009_film) "Star Trek (2009 film)")*.[[33]](#cite_note-ew2009-33) He explained, "Sometimes we'll talk every day. Then there will be a period of a couple weeks where we don't speak. But we're emailing a lot. There's a lot of stuff that happens that way."[[34]](#cite_note-ctvabrams-34) Eden FX and [Zoic Studios](/wiki/Zoic_Studios "Zoic Studios") stayed as vendors for the series' visual effects, effectively giving *Fringe* a sense of continuity. Jay Worth, the overall visual effects supervisor, commented that having these two companies "helped the show not feel as a big of a bump from one season to the next, particularly with different crews and a different vibe a little bit."[[29]](#cite_note-awn1-29) Despite his decreased involvement, Abrams was sent all of the visual effects for the series during production, and responded back with notes and tweaks.[[34]](#cite_note-ctvabrams-34)

### Writing and filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Writing and filming")]

Responding to criticism that some first season episodes were too neatly wrapped and solved, Jeff Pinkner commented towards the end of that season, "We found that, absolutely, early on, we were falling into the trap of—the tease would be fantastic. And then we would too quickly answer it and [reduce] the tension. And we've tried to course-correct and have the tease promise" questions that don't get answered right away.[[35]](#cite_note-chicagotribuneryan-35) He further elaborated that the goal now is to "have the energy of the show get bigger as [an episode] goes along... We're learning how to tell this version of a detective story. It's not really a police procedural. There are elements of that. But it's an incredible mish-mash of genres. I think we're getting better at finding our way through these stories."[[35]](#cite_note-chicagotribuneryan-35)

> "In this season we're really looking to get deeper into our characters and have people really participate with them and watch their evolution, whether it's Walter's emancipation this season and how he's having more of his own awakening. Whether it's Peter in discovering things about himself and about the others he works with and his place in the world. And Olivia the same regard."

– [J.H. Wyman](/wiki/J.H._Wyman "J.H. Wyman")[[36]](#cite_note-blastrbelieve-36)

Pinkner, Abrams, Orci, Burk, Kurtzman, Wyman, and Goldsman developed the second season's storyline together.[[37]](#cite_note-comicbookresources-37) Goldsman explained that they "mapped" out the season "in a way that we remained fundamentally faithful to".[[37]](#cite_note-comicbookresources-37) Though they changed certain aspects, they knew where Olivia and Peter were going to start and end up but "got there at different paces than what [they] originally planned".[[37]](#cite_note-comicbookresources-37) While the first season both focused on Olivia[[38]](#cite_note-latimesevil-38) and dealt with discovering the existence of an enemy, the second was designed to "[build] to a very specific type of confrontation" between the two universes.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Explained Pinkner, "Season 2 is about the people from 'over there' putting the final pieces of the invasion into play, and explaining why".[[33]](#cite_note-ew2009-33) At the same time, the producers approached the second season as a "journey of self-actualization"[[36]](#cite_note-blastrbelieve-36) and "maturation for our characters."[[40]](#cite_note-ctryanobserve-40) They wanted to make the three main cast members become more comfortable as a team in Fringe Division; Peter thus was written to be more heroic and to go from reluctance to a desire to help his father, while Walter became gradually more independent and "grounded as a human being".[[33]](#cite_note-ew2009-33)[[38]](#cite_note-latimesevil-38) They made the secret of Peter's origins one of the main story focuses of the season. While the audience had been made aware of Walter's secret in the season one finale, the producers wanted to "acknowledge it to our characters".[[38]](#cite_note-latimesevil-38) All the while Walter was "suffering... and desperately trying to keep it all quiet", Olivia and Peter grew closer to the truth. By "giv[ing] these things time" to develop, Pinkner strove to avoid focusing just on "event, event, event", instead concentrating on the real consequences of Peter and Olivia's eventual discovery.[[38]](#cite_note-latimesevil-38)

The second season's mythology was intended to be revealed in small parcels.[[33]](#cite_note-ew2009-33) Throughout the season, Pinkner and Wyman tried to create an episode that best bridged "standalone" traits—needed for casual viewers—with the further advancement of the show's mythology for those who watched regularly. At the start of the season Pinkner believed they had found a "good rhythm" between the two, as the mythology "really [started to] affect the characters, to the point where even the standalone [episodes] advance mythology".[[40]](#cite_note-ctryanobserve-40) After the season ended, Wyman commented at [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con"), "We learned that the fan fans love mythology but there are needs from the networks. We started experimenting with a thing we called 'mythalones', not standalone or myth."[[41]](#cite_note-ugocomiccon-41) Wyman cited the season's 18th episode, "[White Tulip](/wiki/White_Tulip "White Tulip")" as a prime example, noting "You can see that you're following Walter's journey, it's the right mix."[[41]](#cite_note-ugocomiccon-41) The *Fringe* producers strove to avoid becoming bogged down in mythology, an issue that they perceived happened in *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")*, another J. J. Abrams television series. In a joint statement released midway through the season, Abrams, Pinkner, and Wyman noted that their "only internal rule is that we make every effort to not raise mythological questions merely to string viewers along, but rather to provide answers that generate consequences."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

At Fox's request, the writers developed a musical episode, "[Brown Betty](/wiki/Brown_Betty_(Fringe) "Brown Betty (Fringe)")" to fit into the network's "Fox rocks" campaign in the same vein as its series *[Glee](/wiki/Glee_(TV_series) "Glee (TV series)")*.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-ts-44)[[45]](#cite_note-newscorppressrelease-45) While the episode already had most of the necessary elements in place before Fox's request, the producers were able to add the musical theme as a "narrative device" to "explore Walter's feelings" in the aftermath of Peter's discovery and flight.[[46]](#cite_note-46) To prepare for the season finale, the producers began developing characteristics of the parallel universe relatively early on. Relating to the two universes' idiosyncrasies, Pinkner and Wyman were both interested in world building and "the concept of choices", such as the differing events that led to the Other Side possessing a still-standing [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") but destroyed [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House").[[38]](#cite_note-latimesevil-38)[[47]](#cite_note-usaweekendworldbuilding-47) Other historical idiosyncrasies included the [oxidation](/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation") of the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") and the use of [zeppelins](/wiki/Zeppelin "Zeppelin") as transportation.[[38]](#cite_note-latimesevil-38) They believed that all of their additions were "the texture that actually makes it a world. The richness of detail is what makes it feel real".[[47]](#cite_note-usaweekendworldbuilding-47)

As the series began filming in Vancouver, the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia") campus served as a substitute for [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Because of its heritage buildings and antique storefronts, many of the scenes set in the alternate universe were shot in [New Westminster](/wiki/New_Westminster "New Westminster"), an area outside Vancouver.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Consulting producer and previous *Fringe* director Akiva Goldsman returned to direct several episodes, as did producer [Brad Anderson](/wiki/Brad_Anderson_(director) "Brad Anderson (director)"), executive producer [Joe Chappelle](/wiki/Joe_Chappelle "Joe Chappelle"), and producer Paul Edwards. One time guest directors for the season included [Bryan Spicer](/wiki/Bryan_Spicer "Bryan Spicer"),[[50]](#cite_note-episodefracture-50) [Jon Cassar](/wiki/Jon_Cassar "Jon Cassar"),[[51]](#cite_note-earthling-51) [Dennis Smith](/wiki/Dennis_Smith_(director) "Dennis Smith (director)"),[[52]](#cite_note-episodeaugust-52) [Paul Holahan](/wiki/Paul_Holahan "Paul Holahan"),[[53]](#cite_note-snakehead-53) [Jeannot Szwarc](/wiki/Jeannot_Szwarc "Jeannot Szwarc"),[[54]](#cite_note-54) [Frederick E. O. Toye](/wiki/Fred_Toye "Fred Toye"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-56) [Deran Sarafian](/wiki/Deran_Sarafian "Deran Sarafian"),[[56]](#cite_note-whatliesbelow-57) [Adam Davidson](/wiki/Adam_Davidson_(director) "Adam Davidson (director)"),[[57]](#cite_note-bishoprevival-58) [Charles Beeson](/wiki/Charles_Beeson_(director) "Charles Beeson (director)"),[[58]](#cite_note-59) [David Straiton](/wiki/David_Straiton "David Straiton"),[[59]](#cite_note-episodepeter-60) [Thomas Yatsko](/wiki/Thomas_Yatsko "Thomas Yatsko"),[[60]](#cite_note-61) Jeffrey Hunt,[[61]](#cite_note-62) and [Seith Mann](/wiki/Seith_Mann "Seith Mann").[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cast")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Leonard_Nimoy_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Leonard_Nimoy_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonard_Nimoy_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

Guest actor [Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy") appeared in four episodes in the second season.

#### Main cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Main cast")]

As with the previous season, the second season featured three main characters all working together to solve various Fringe cases. [Anna Torv](/wiki/Anna_Torv "Anna Torv") played determined FBI agent [Olivia Dunham](/wiki/Olivia_Dunham "Olivia Dunham"), who is able to travel between universes as a result of childhood experiments performed on her with the [nootropic drug](/wiki/Nootropic_drug "Nootropic drug"), Cortexiphan. The man responsible for these experiments, [Dr. Walter Bishop](/wiki/Walter_Bishop_(Fringe) "Walter Bishop (Fringe)"), was played by [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble"). Walter's son [Peter Bishop](/wiki/Peter_Bishop "Peter Bishop"), whom he stole from the parallel universe, was portrayed by [Joshua Jackson](/wiki/Joshua_Jackson "Joshua Jackson").[[33]](#cite_note-ew2009-33)[[63]](#cite_note-64) Other members of the main cast included [Jasika Nicole](/wiki/Jasika_Nicole "Jasika Nicole") as Junior FBI Agent and Walter's lab assistant Astrid Farnsworth, [Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick") as Agent Phillip Broyles, [Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown") as Massive Dynamic executive Nina Sharp, and [Kirk Acevedo](/wiki/Kirk_Acevedo "Kirk Acevedo") as Agent Charlie Francis.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Acevedo's character was killed off in the season's [fourth episode](/wiki/Momentum_Deferred "Momentum Deferred"), though at the time Pinkner and Wyman hinted of the actor's possible return later in the season.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Acevedo returned to guest star in the [season finale](/wiki/Over_There_(Fringe) "Over There (Fringe)") as the parallel universe version of Charlie Francis.[[66]](#cite_note-67) Acevedo was also featured in "[Unearthed](/wiki/Unearthed_(Fringe) "Unearthed (Fringe)")", a special episode that was filmed during the first season,[[67]](#cite_note-68) but aired as the eleventh episode of the second season.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Ratings and broadcast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ratings and broadcast")]

*Fringe*'s first season ended with an average of 9.96 million viewers,[[98]](#cite_note-100) and among the season's new series, it was the first rated show for adults 18–49.[[26]](#cite_note-ctribunemadmen-26)[[99]](#cite_note-ignrenewal-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102) On May 4, 2009, a week before the [season one finale](/wiki/There%27s_More_Than_One_of_Everything "There's More Than One of Everything"), Fox renewed *Fringe* for a second season, giving it a full season pick-up of 22 episodes.[[101]](#cite_note-103) The network's president of entertainment, [Kevin Reilly](/wiki/Kevin_Reilly_(executive) "Kevin Reilly (executive)"), explained "*Fringe* proved to be a notable addition to our schedule all season and it really has fans buzzing as it builds to a fantastic season finale."[[26]](#cite_note-ctribunemadmen-26)[[99]](#cite_note-ignrenewal-101)

Later in May, Fox announced *Fringe* would be moving from Tuesdays to Thursdays for the second season, to be aired in the competitive 9:00 pm timeslot.[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-hollywoodreporteryreilly-105) Kevin Reilly explained the move, "The door is more open on this night than it has been in a long time. *Fringe* is a real alternative to both [*[Grey's](/wiki/Grey%27s_Anatomy "Grey's Anatomy")* and *[CSI: Crime Scene Investigation](/wiki/CSI:_Crime_Scene_Investigation "CSI: Crime Scene Investigation")*]."[[103]](#cite_note-hollywoodreporteryreilly-105) However, after a perceived "healthy" first season,[[104]](#cite_note-digitalspyratings-106) the second season premiere was watched by an estimated 7.817 million viewers.[[105]](#cite_note-107) Ratings for the season continued to decline, culminating in 5.68 million viewers watching the finale.[[104]](#cite_note-digitalspyratings-106) *Fringe*'s second season ended with an average of 6.252 million viewers per episode and a 2.3 ratings share for adults 18–49, causing the series to finish in 79th place out of all the season's network television shows.[[106]](#cite_note-108) However, *Fringe* and its lead-in, *[Bones](/wiki/Bones_(TV_series) "Bones (TV series)")*, did help the network increase 52% among adults aged 18–49 and 65 percent among total viewers from the same night the previous year.[[107]](#cite_note-109) Despite its middling ratings, *Fringe* received a full-season renewal on March 6, 2010.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

### Reviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Reviews")]

The second season of *Fringe* received generally very favorable reviews. At the beginning of the season, the series was featured on the September 18 cover of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*, which promised to give readers a "deep dive into the gory, witty world of Fox's *Fringe*".[[109]](#cite_note-111) On [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the season has an approval rating of 81% with an average score of 8.2 out of 10 based on 16 reviews. The website's critical consensus reads, "*Fringe* surpasses expectations in season two with stronger character development while maintaining its creepy sci-fi angle."[[110]](#cite_note-112) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), a review aggregate website, gave the second season 74/100 based upon ten reviews, indicating a "generally favorable" reception.[[111]](#cite_note-113) After viewing the first eight episodes, *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* columnist Ken Tucker gave the series an A−, calling it "one of the fastest, smartest, wittiest shows on television now... *Fringe* successfully mixes the crime genre with sci-fi, and cold conspiracies with heartfelt emotion."[[112]](#cite_note-ewreview8-114) Peter Swanson from *[Slant Magazine](/wiki/Slant_Magazine "Slant Magazine")* gave *Fringe* two and a half stars after watching the first six episodes. He believed the second season had "floundered a little, stuck in that creative hinterland between the desire to grab new viewers and the need to build on the mythology of the show's universe."[[113]](#cite_note-slantseason-115) While Swanson understood the need to attract a larger audience, he thought the standalone episodes to be "less than stellar", as "they've yet to get scary, or even vaguely unsettling".[[113]](#cite_note-slantseason-115) Swanson did however find the war between two universes to be "particularly compelling", and expressed hope that "*Fringe* [would] find its footing."[[113]](#cite_note-slantseason-115)

In a review of the entire second season, [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") believed that despite the premiere's "great start", the first half contained "some rather lackluster episodes" that "made a lot of fans worried and got the rumors of cancellation circulating"; the second half however "showed that *Fringe* is still one of the best sci-fi series on TV".[[114]](#cite_note-ignseasonreview-116) [The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club") agreed, and called the first half "entertaining" but "never essential" while noting a great improvement mid-way through the season, as "the show finally grounded its freak-of-the-week weirdness in deep sadness".[[115]](#cite_note-avclubbest25-117) The A.V. Club continued, "...The season-two episode "[Peter](/wiki/Peter_(Fringe_episode) "Peter (Fringe episode)")" finally dramatized the moment that changed [Peter's] life, giving the series' overarching storyline a devastating emotional core, based in a father's love instead of in theoretical concepts. It only got better from there, as the series expanded its world by further making those concepts concrete. *Fringe* is that rare blend of inventive ideas, wild ambition, and unexpected soulfulness."[[115]](#cite_note-avclubbest25-117) The *[Seattle Post-Intelligencer](/wiki/Seattle_Post-Intelligencer "Seattle Post-Intelligencer")* believed that by the end of the season "*Fringe* had truly found its footing, doing daring, experimental episodes like the musical "[Brown Betty](/wiki/Brown_Betty_(Fringe) "Brown Betty (Fringe)")"... and heartbreaking stand alone episodes, like "[White Tulip](/wiki/White_Tulip "White Tulip")", (which might be my favorite hour of television this year that wasn't *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)")*-related)."[[116]](#cite_note-seattlepi-118)

In particular, critics highlighted the season premiere "[A New Day in the Old Town](/wiki/A_New_Day_in_the_Old_Town "A New Day in the Old Town")"[[114]](#cite_note-ignseasonreview-116) as well as regular episodes "Peter"[[115]](#cite_note-avclubbest25-117)[[117]](#cite_note-119)[[118]](#cite_note-ignbestscifi-120) and "White Tulip",[[114]](#cite_note-ignseasonreview-116)[[115]](#cite_note-avclubbest25-117)[[116]](#cite_note-seattlepi-118) and the season finale "[Over There](/wiki/Over_There_(Fringe) "Over There (Fringe)")".[[118]](#cite_note-ignbestscifi-120)[[119]](#cite_note-digitalspy-121) The main three cast members' performances were praised,[[112]](#cite_note-ewreview8-114) and various critics noted the series continued the sense of humor seen in the first season.[[112]](#cite_note-ewreview8-114)[[120]](#cite_note-122) *Fringe*'s second season was chosen for a number of 2010 "best of television" lists, including *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*,[[121]](#cite_note-123) *Entertainment Weekly*,[[122]](#cite_note-124) [Digital Spy](/wiki/Digital_Spy "Digital Spy"),[[119]](#cite_note-digitalspy-121) *[The Daily Beast](/wiki/The_Daily_Beast "The Daily Beast")*,[[123]](#cite_note-125) the *[Seattle Post-Intelligencer](/wiki/Seattle_Post-Intelligencer "Seattle Post-Intelligencer")*,[[124]](#cite_note-126) [AOL](/wiki/AOL "AOL")'s [TV Squad](/wiki/TV_Squad "TV Squad"),[[125]](#cite_note-127) as well as IGN, which named *Fringe* the best sci-fi series of 2010, beating fellow nominees *Lost*, *[Caprica](/wiki/Caprica_(TV_series) "Caprica (TV series)")*, and *[Stargate Universe](/wiki/Stargate_Universe "Stargate Universe")*.[[126]](#cite_note-128)

### Awards and nominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Awards and nominations")]

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Fringe](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Fringe "List of awards and nominations received by Fringe")

At the [62nd Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/62nd_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "62nd Primetime Emmy Awards"), actor [Joshua Jackson](/wiki/Joshua_Jackson "Joshua Jackson") and actress [Anna Torv](/wiki/Anna_Torv "Anna Torv") submitted their work in the second season for consideration in the [Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Lead_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series") and [Outstanding Lead Actress in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Lead_Actress_in_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Drama Series") categories, respectively. Actors [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble"), [Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick"), [Kirk Acevedo](/wiki/Kirk_Acevedo "Kirk Acevedo"), and actress [Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown") submitted their work for consideration in the [Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Supporting_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series") and [Outstanding Supporting Actress in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Supporting_Actress_in_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actress in a Drama Series") categories, respectively.[[127]](#cite_note-129) The second season of *Fringe* was submitted for consideration in the [Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series") category[[128]](#cite_note-130) but failed to garner a nomination. The failure of the series to garner any major category nominations at the Emmys was perceived as a notable snub by many media outlets.[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133)[[132]](#cite_note-134)

The second season received nominations for Sound Editing at the [Creative Arts Emmy Awards](/wiki/Creative_Arts_Emmy_Award "Creative Arts Emmy Award")[[133]](#cite_note-135) and at the [Golden Reel Awards](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Sound_Editors "Motion Picture Sound Editors").[[134]](#cite_note-136) The season's sole musical episode, "[Brown Betty](/wiki/Brown_Betty_(Fringe) "Brown Betty (Fringe)")", received a nomination for Short Form Musical In Television at the Golden Reel Awards.[[135]](#cite_note-137) Noble, Torv, and guest actor [Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy") were nominated at the 2010 [Saturn Awards](/wiki/Saturn_Award "Saturn Award"), with Torv and Nimoy winning in their respective categories.[[136]](#cite_note-138) Noble also received a nomination at the 2010 [Satellite Awards](/wiki/Satellite_Award "Satellite Award"), but lost to *[Dexter](/wiki/Dexter_(TV_series) "Dexter (TV series)")*'s [John Lithgow](/wiki/John_Lithgow "John Lithgow").[[137]](#cite_note-139)

## Home video releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Home video releases")]

The second season of *Fringe* was released on DVD and Blu-ray in region 1 on September 14, 2010,[[138]](#cite_note-140) in region 2 on September 27, 2010[[139]](#cite_note-141) and in region 4 on November 10, 2010.[[140]](#cite_note-142) The sets includes all 22 episodes (plus an unaired episode) of season two on a 6-disc DVD set and a 4-disc Blu-ray set presented in anamorphic widescreen. Special features on the sets include four commentary tracks—"Momentum Deferred" with Jill Risk, [Matthew Pitts](/wiki/Matthew_Pitts "Matthew Pitts"), Danielle Dispaltro, Justin Doble and Charles Scott IV; "Peter" with [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble"), [Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown") and Damian Holbrook; "Brown Betty" with Tanya Swerling, Billy Gottlieb, [Chris Tilton](/wiki/Chris_Tilton "Chris Tilton") and Jay Worth; and "Over There, Part 2" with [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner"), [J. H. Wyman](/wiki/J._H._Wyman "J. H. Wyman") and [Akiva Goldsman](/wiki/Akiva_Goldsman "Akiva Goldsman").[[141]](#cite_note-monstersandcriticsdvd-143) Episodic behind-the-scene featurettes include "Analyzing the Scene" on six episodes, "Dissected Files: Unaired Scenes" on select episodes and "Unusual Side Effects: Gag Reel". Other featurettes include "In the Lab with John Noble and Prop Master Rob Smith" and "Beyond the Pattern: The Mythology of *Fringe*".[[141]](#cite_note-monstersandcriticsdvd-143)[[142]](#cite_note-igndvd-144) The unaired episode from season one, "[Unearthed](/wiki/Unearthed_(Fringe) "Unearthed (Fringe)")", is presented as a special feature, separate from the other episodes.[[142]](#cite_note-igndvd-144)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_season_2&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-56)** Note that Toye directed the episode during first season production.[[55]](#cite_note-unearthed-55)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-77)** Nimoy appeared in three of the season's episodes and despite announcing his retirement[[74]](#cite_note-75) agreed to guest star in the season premiere as well.[[75]](#cite_note-76) According to Jeff Pinkner, "We had a story for William that we got to tell. Leonard has retired twice, he's our [Bret Farve](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*]. We had him in and talked him through where we wanted it to end in season two and he said 'ok, I'll come back.' He was so unbelievably gracious. [The role] was not created with him in mind but it was the perfect mix. He was nervous that we just wanted him for promotional reasons but we wanted to give him a swan song role."[[41]](#cite_note-ugocomiccon-41)
